name: museum_provenance_dispute_innocent_current_holder
premise: Museum director Casey Carr must decide whether to remove a prized textile from display after new provenance research suggests forced sale during occupation, though the current donor family bought it in good faith at auction decades ago. General counsel Evan Park warns immediate removal could tank a capital campaign tied to the gallery wing. Delaying restitution talks angers survivor communities planning a protest at opening night.

agents.name: Casey Carr
agents.identity: I am the museum director accountable to ethical acquisitions codes, donor relationships, and survivor community partnerships.
agents.goal: I want wrongs acknowledged without publicly shaming a good-faith family before confidential mediation concludes.

agents.name: Evan Park
agents.identity: I am general counsel who evaluates title defects, publicity risk, and potential seizure requests from source countries.
agents.goal: I want the piece off public display immediately to reduce fraudulent-good-faith arguments if litigation begins.

choices: I will remove the textile from display immediately and replace it with interpretive panels about ongoing provenance review, accepting donor relationship strain during the campaign.
I will keep the textile on display with expanded labeling about contested provenance and freeze acquisition signatures until mediation ends, accepting protest optics at opening night.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Opening night is forty-eight hours away and donors are arriving for previews. Casey Carr must choose between immediate removal with panels or on-wall display with expanded labeling. What does Casey Carr do?
